\begin{document}

\begin{center}
    {\LARGE \bfseries Boundary Value Problems and Sturm--Liouville Theory}\\[6pt]
    {\large Prepared by Staples Education}\\[4pt]
    {\itshape Unit 17: Eigenfunctions, Orthogonality, and the Functional Basis}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.4em}

\tableofcontents
\vspace{0.4em}

\begin{topicsbox}[Unit 17 at a Glance: From Side Conditions to a Functional Basis]
An \textcolor{accentorange}{initial} value problem pins a solution down with all its side data at one point; a \textcolor{accentorange}{boundary} value problem spreads that data across two endpoints, and existence and uniqueness are no longer guaranteed. The interesting case is the homogeneous eigenvalue BVP $X''+\lambda X=0$, which is silent (only $X\equiv 0$) except at a discrete ladder of \textcolor{accentorange}{eigenvalues} $\lambda_n$, where it rings with \textcolor{accentorange}{eigenfunctions} $X_n$. Those eigenfunctions are mutually \emph{orthogonal}, and orthogonality is the master key: it turns the infinite expansion $f=\sum c_n y_n$ into a list of one-line coefficient integrals. The \textcolor{accentorange}{Sturm--Liouville} form $(p\,y')'+(q+\lambda w)y=0$ packages every such problem and guarantees the eigenfunctions form a complete, weighted-orthogonal basis.

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=1.4em, itemsep=3pt]
    \item \textbf{IVP vs.\ BVP} --- moving the side conditions to two endpoints can make a linear problem have \emph{no} solution, \emph{one}, or \emph{infinitely many}.
    \item \textbf{Eigenvalues / eigenfunctions} --- $X''+\lambda X=0$, $X(0)=X(L)=0$ is nontrivial only at $\lambda_n=(n\pi/L)^2$ with $X_n=\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}$.
    \item \textbf{The shooting method} --- recast a BVP as an IVP with a \emph{guessed} initial slope, then adjust the slope until the far boundary condition is met.
    \item \textbf{Orthogonality} --- $\int_a^b y_n y_m\,w\,dx=0$ for $n\neq m$ makes each coefficient $c_n=\langle f,y_n\rangle/\langle y_n,y_n\rangle$ an independent integral.
    \item \textbf{Sturm--Liouville} --- $(p\,y')'+(q+\lambda w)y=0$ has real eigenvalues $\lambda_n\to\infty$ and $w$-orthogonal eigenfunctions that form a complete basis $f=\sum c_n y_n$.
\end{itemize}
\end{topicsbox}

\vspace{0.6em}

\setcounter{section}{17}

% ---- Module 17.1 --------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Initial versus Boundary Value Problems}
\emph{Side conditions at one point (IVP) versus two endpoints (BVP); Dirichlet ($y$), Neumann ($y'$), and Robin conditions; the loss of the existence--uniqueness guarantee; steady-state heat conduction as the model BVP.}

\begin{formulabox}[IVP versus BVP --- Where the Data Lives]
\[ \textbf{IVP:}\ \ y''=F(x,y,y'),\ \ y(x_0)=y_0,\ y'(x_0)=y_0' \quad\text{(all data at one point } x_0); \qquad \textbf{BVP:}\ \ y(a)=\alpha,\ y(b)=\beta \quad\text{(data split across } a,b). \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[The Three Standard Boundary Conditions]
\[ \text{Dirichlet: } y(a)=\alpha \ \text{(fixes the value)}; \quad \text{Neumann: } y'(a)=\alpha \ \text{(fixes the slope/flux)}; \quad \text{Robin: } c_1 y(a)+c_2 y'(a)=\alpha. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Existence and Uniqueness --- The Contrast]
\[ \text{A linear IVP (continuous coefficients)} \Rightarrow \textbf{exactly one} \text{ solution}; \qquad \text{a linear BVP} \Rightarrow \textbf{none, one, or infinitely many}. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{methodbox}[Method --- Solve a Linear Two-Point BVP]
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.6em]
    \item Find the general solution of the ODE, carrying both arbitrary constants $c_1,c_2$.
    \item Impose the condition at the left endpoint $x=a$ to get one equation in $c_1,c_2$.
    \item Impose the condition at the right endpoint $x=b$ to get a second equation.
    \item Solve the $2\times 2$ system: a unique $(c_1,c_2)$ gives one solution; an inconsistent system gives none; a dependent system gives infinitely many.
\end{enumerate}
\end{methodbox}

\begin{examplebox}[Worked Example --- A Simple Dirichlet BVP]
Solve $y''=0$ on $[0,2]$ with $y(0)=1$ and $y(2)=5$.
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{General solution: } y''=0 \Rightarrow y(x)=c_1+c_2 x.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Left BC: } y(0)=c_1=1 \Rightarrow c_1=1.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Right BC: } y(2)=c_1+2c_2=5 \Rightarrow 1+2c_2=5 \Rightarrow c_2=2.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Unique solution: } y(x)=1+2x \ \text{(a single straight line meeting both endpoints).}} \]
\end{examplebox}

\begin{conceptbox}[Key Takeaway]
An initial value problem stacks all its side data at \textcolor{accentorange}{one} point and is rewarded with a unique solution; a boundary value problem spreads the data across \textcolor{accentorange}{two} endpoints and forfeits that guarantee. The same ODE can then have no solution, one, or infinitely many --- which is exactly what makes BVPs the natural home for eigenvalues.
\end{conceptbox}

\begin{warningbox}[Common Pitfall]
Do not carry the IVP intuition --- ``a second-order equation always has a unique solution given two conditions'' --- into BVPs. \textcolor{warnred}{Two boundary conditions need not pin down a unique solution.} Whether you get none, one, or infinitely many depends on how the conditions interact with the general solution, not merely on counting constants.
\end{warningbox}

\vspace{0.8em}

% ---- Module 17.2 --------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Two-Point Boundary Value Problems}
\emph{The standard solve-then-impose method for linear two-point BVPs; the three possible outcomes; homogeneous BVPs and the omnipresent trivial solution; nonhomogeneous BVPs with forcing.}

\begin{formulabox}[The Linear Two-Point BVP]
\[ y'' + P(x)y' + Q(x)y = R(x), \qquad y(a)=\alpha,\quad y(b)=\beta; \qquad y=c_1 y_1+c_2 y_2+y_p \ \text{before imposing the BCs.} \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Three Possible Outcomes]
\[ \det\begin{pmatrix} y_1(a) & y_2(a) \\ y_1(b) & y_2(b) \end{pmatrix} \neq 0 \ \Rightarrow\ \textbf{unique}; \qquad =0 \ \Rightarrow\ \textbf{none or } \infty\text{-many (depending on } \alpha,\beta). \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Homogeneous BVP --- The Trivial Solution]
\[ y''+\lambda y=0,\ \ y(0)=y(L)=0 \ \Rightarrow\ y\equiv 0 \ \text{ always solves it; nontrivial } y \text{ exist only at special } \lambda. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{methodbox}[Method --- Resolve a Two-Point BVP]
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.6em]
    \item Solve the ODE completely, including a particular solution $y_p$ if the equation is nonhomogeneous.
    \item Substitute the two boundary conditions to obtain a linear system in $c_1,c_2$.
    \item Compute the boundary determinant: nonzero means a unique solution exists --- solve for $c_1,c_2$.
    \item If the determinant is zero, test consistency: an impossible equation gives no solution, a redundant one gives a one-parameter family of solutions.
\end{enumerate}
\end{methodbox}

\begin{examplebox}[Worked Example --- A Nonhomogeneous BVP]
Solve $y''=2$ on $[0,1]$ with $y(0)=0$ and $y(1)=0$.
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{General solution: } y''=2 \Rightarrow y'=2x+c_2 \Rightarrow y(x)=x^2+c_2 x+c_1.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Left BC: } y(0)=c_1=0 \Rightarrow c_1=0.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Right BC: } y(1)=1+c_2+0=0 \Rightarrow c_2=-1.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Unique solution: } y(x)=x^2-x \ \text{(a downward parabola pinned to } 0 \text{ at both ends).}} \]
\end{examplebox}

\begin{conceptbox}[Key Takeaway]
Every linear two-point BVP is solved the same way: find the \textcolor{accentorange}{general solution first}, then let the two boundary conditions decide the constants. The boundary determinant is the referee --- nonzero awards a unique solution, while zero throws the problem into the no-solution or infinitely-many camp, the very fork that produces eigenvalues.
\end{conceptbox}

\begin{warningbox}[Common Pitfall]
Never impose a boundary condition before you have the \emph{full} general solution (including $y_p$). \textcolor{warnred}{Dropping a homogeneous piece or the particular solution} silently discards the freedom the BCs need, and you will report a unique answer where the true problem has none or infinitely many.
\end{warningbox}

\vspace{0.8em}

% ---- Module 17.3 --------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Eigenvalues and Eigenfunctions of Boundary Value Problems}
\emph{The eigenvalue BVP $X''+\lambda X=0$ with homogeneous BCs; the three-case analysis $\lambda<0,\ \lambda=0,\ \lambda>0$; Dirichlet eigenvalues $\lambda_n=(n\pi/L)^2$ with eigenfunctions $\sin(n\pi x/L)$; the Neumann variant with cosines.}

\begin{formulabox}[The Eigenvalue Boundary Value Problem]
\[ X'' + \lambda X = 0, \qquad X(0)=0,\ X(L)=0; \qquad \lambda \text{ is an \emph{eigenvalue} if a nontrivial } X \text{ (the \emph{eigenfunction}) exists.} \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Dirichlet Spectrum]
\[ \lambda_n = \left(\frac{n\pi}{L}\right)^2, \qquad X_n(x)=\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}, \qquad n=1,2,3,\dots \quad (\lambda_n\to\infty). \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Neumann Spectrum]
\[ X'(0)=X'(L)=0:\quad \lambda_n=\left(\frac{n\pi}{L}\right)^2,\quad X_n(x)=\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L},\quad n=0,1,2,\dots \ (\lambda_0=0 \text{ allowed}, X_0=1). \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{methodbox}[Method --- Find the Eigenvalues and Eigenfunctions]
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.6em]
    \item Split into three cases by the sign of $\lambda$, since the form of the general solution differs for $\lambda<0$, $\lambda=0$, and $\lambda>0$.
    \item For $\lambda<0$ (write $\lambda=-\mu^2$) and $\lambda=0$, impose the BCs and show only the trivial solution survives --- no negative or zero eigenvalues here.
    \item For $\lambda>0$ (write $\lambda=\mu^2$) use $X=A\cos\mu x+B\sin\mu x$; the left BC kills $A$, and the right BC forces $\sin\mu L=0$.
    \item Solve the quantization condition $\mu L=n\pi$ to read off $\lambda_n=(n\pi/L)^2$ and the eigenfunctions $X_n=\sin(n\pi x/L)$.
\end{enumerate}
\end{methodbox}

\begin{examplebox}[Worked Example --- Full Three-Case Analysis on $0\le x\le \pi$]
Find all eigenvalues and eigenfunctions of $X''+\lambda X=0$ with $X(0)=0$, $X(\pi)=0$.
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Case } \lambda<0\ (\lambda=-\mu^2):\ X=A\cosh\mu x+B\sinh\mu x;\ X(0)=A=0,\ X(\pi)=B\sinh\mu\pi=0\Rightarrow B=0.\ \text{Only } X\equiv 0.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Case } \lambda=0:\ X=A+Bx;\ X(0)=A=0,\ X(\pi)=B\pi=0\Rightarrow B=0.\ \text{Only } X\equiv 0.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Case } \lambda>0\ (\lambda=\mu^2):\ X=A\cos\mu x+B\sin\mu x;\ X(0)=A=0 \Rightarrow X=B\sin\mu x.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Quantization: } X(\pi)=B\sin\mu\pi=0,\ B\neq 0 \Rightarrow \sin\mu\pi=0 \Rightarrow \mu\pi=n\pi \Rightarrow \mu=n.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Spectrum: } \lambda_n=n^2,\quad X_n(x)=\sin(nx),\quad n=1,2,3,\dots} \]
\end{examplebox}

\begin{conceptbox}[Key Takeaway]
A homogeneous BVP is a \textcolor{accentorange}{filter}: it passes only the trivial solution except at a discrete ladder of eigenvalues $\lambda_n=(n\pi/L)^2$, where it resonates with the eigenfunctions $\sin(n\pi x/L)$. The negative and zero cases always collapse to $X\equiv 0$; the entire spectrum lives in $\lambda>0$ and marches off to infinity.
\end{conceptbox}

\begin{warningbox}[Common Pitfall]
You \textcolor{warnred}{must check all three sign cases} of $\lambda$ --- skipping the $\lambda\le 0$ analysis is only safe once you have \emph{shown} it yields nothing. Also, $n=0$ gives $X\equiv 0$ for Dirichlet (so start at $n=1$), but $n=0$ \textcolor{warnred}{is} a genuine eigenvalue $\lambda_0=0$ with $X_0=1$ in the Neumann case.
\end{warningbox}

\vspace{0.8em}

% ---- Module 17.4 --------------------------------------------------
\subsection{The Shooting Method}
\emph{Recasting a BVP as an IVP with a guessed initial slope $s$; the boundary-mismatch function $F(s)=y(b;s)-\beta$; root-finding on $F$; for linear BVPs, exactly two shots plus linear interpolation; reuse of Runge--Kutta integrators.}

\begin{formulabox}[BVP Recast as a Parameterized IVP]
\[ y''=f(x,y,y'),\ y(a)=\alpha,\ y(b)=\beta \ \longrightarrow\ y''=f,\ y(a)=\alpha,\ y'(a)=s \ (\text{guess the slope } s). \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[The Boundary-Mismatch Function]
\[ F(s) = y(b;\,s) - \beta, \qquad \text{the shooting method seeks a root } F(s)=0 \ \text{so the far BC is met.} \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Linear BVP --- Two Shots and Interpolate]
\[ F \text{ affine in } s \Rightarrow s^\star = s_0 + (s_1-s_0)\,\frac{\beta - y(b;s_0)}{y(b;s_1)-y(b;s_0)} \ \text{(exact after two shots).} \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{methodbox}[Method --- Shoot a Boundary Value Problem]
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.6em]
    \item Convert the BVP to an IVP by replacing the far condition $y(b)=\beta$ with a guessed slope $y'(a)=s$.
    \item Integrate the IVP numerically (e.g.\ Runge--Kutta) from $a$ to $b$ and read off the overshoot/undershoot $F(s)=y(b;s)-\beta$.
    \item Adjust $s$ to drive $F(s)\to 0$: bisection or Newton in general; for a \emph{linear} BVP, two shots fix the affine $F$ exactly.
    \item Interpolate to the exact slope $s^\star$ and integrate once more to produce the solution that meets both boundary conditions.
\end{enumerate}
\end{methodbox}

\begin{examplebox}[Worked Example --- Two Shots on a Linear BVP]
Solve $y''=y$ on $[0,1]$ with $y(0)=0$, $y(1)=1$ by shooting (the problem is linear, so two shots suffice).
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Recast: } y''=y,\ y(0)=0,\ y'(0)=s;\ \text{the exact IVP solution is } y(x;s)=s\sinh x.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Shot } s_0=0:\ y(1;0)=0 \Rightarrow F(0)=0-1=-1.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Shot } s_1=1:\ y(1;1)=\sinh 1\approx 1.1752 \Rightarrow F(1)=1.1752-1=0.1752.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Interpolate: } s^\star=0+(1-0)\,\dfrac{1-0}{\,1.1752-0\,}=\dfrac{1}{\sinh 1}\approx 0.8509.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Final shot: } y(x)=\dfrac{\sinh x}{\sinh 1},\ \text{which satisfies } y(0)=0,\ y(1)=1 \text{ exactly.}} \]
\end{examplebox}

\begin{conceptbox}[Key Takeaway]
The shooting method trades a hard BVP for a familiar IVP by \textcolor{accentorange}{guessing the missing initial slope} and then correcting it until the far boundary condition is hit. Root-finding on the mismatch $F(s)=y(b;s)-\beta$ does the correcting --- and because $F$ is affine for a linear BVP, \textcolor{accentorange}{two shots and a straight-line interpolation} land it exactly, all while reusing standard Runge--Kutta machinery.
\end{conceptbox}

\begin{warningbox}[Common Pitfall]
The clean two-shot interpolation is a \textcolor{warnred}{linear-problem privilege}. For a nonlinear BVP $F(s)$ is curved, so two shots do not suffice --- you must iterate (Newton/bisection), and the IVP can be \textcolor{warnred}{unstable}, amplifying small slope errors over $[a,b]$ and ruining the far value.
\end{warningbox}

\vspace{0.8em}

% ---- Module 17.5 --------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Orthogonal Functions}
\emph{The inner product $\langle f,g\rangle=\int_a^b fg\,dx$; orthogonality as a vanishing inner product; the sine orthogonality integral ($0$ for $m\neq n$, $L/2$ for $m=n$); the functional basis and the generalized Fourier coefficient $c_n=\langle f,y_n\rangle/\langle y_n,y_n\rangle$.}

\begin{formulabox}[Inner Product and Orthogonality]
\[ \langle f,g\rangle = \int_a^b f(x)\,g(x)\,dx; \qquad f\perp g \iff \langle f,g\rangle = 0 \ \text{(weighted: } \langle f,g\rangle_w=\int_a^b fg\,w\,dx). \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[The Sine Orthogonality Integral]
\[ \int_0^L \sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\sin\frac{m\pi x}{L}\,dx = \begin{cases} 0, & m\neq n, \\[2pt] \dfrac{L}{2}, & m=n. \end{cases} \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Functional Basis --- Generalized Fourier Expansion]
\[ f(x) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} c_n\, y_n(x), \qquad c_n = \frac{\langle f,\,y_n\rangle_w}{\langle y_n,\,y_n\rangle_w} = \frac{\displaystyle\int_a^b f\,y_n\,w\,dx}{\displaystyle\int_a^b y_n^2\,w\,dx}. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{methodbox}[Method --- Expand a Function in an Eigenfunction Series]
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.6em]
    \item Identify the orthogonal eigenfunctions $\{y_n\}$ and the weight $w$ for the underlying BVP (for a string, $y_n=\sin(n\pi x/L)$, $w=1$).
    \item Write the target expansion $f(x)=\sum_n c_n y_n(x)$ and isolate a single coefficient by taking the inner product of both sides with $y_m$.
    \item Apply orthogonality: every term with $n\neq m$ vanishes, collapsing the infinite sum to one surviving term $c_m\langle y_m,y_m\rangle$.
    \item Solve $c_m=\langle f,y_m\rangle/\langle y_m,y_m\rangle$ by evaluating the numerator and the (known) normalization integral in full.
\end{enumerate}
\end{methodbox}

\begin{examplebox}[Worked Example --- Vibrating String: Eigenfunction Expansion of $f(x)=x$]
A string of length $L$ is fixed at both ends, so its spatial modes solve the eigenvalue BVP $X''+\lambda X=0$, $X(0)=X(L)=0$. (1)~Set up the operator and BVP, (2)~find the eigenvalues and eigenfunctions, (3)~build the coefficient formula from orthogonality, and (4)~expand the initial profile $f(x)=x$ on $[0,L]$ as $f(x)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}c_n\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}$, computing every integral in full.
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Step 1 --- Operator and BVP.}\quad \mathcal{L}[X]=-X''=\lambda X,\quad X(0)=X(L)=0:\ \text{a regular Sturm--Liouville problem with } p=1,\ q=0,\ w=1.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Step 2 --- Eigenvalues.}\quad \text{Only } \lambda=\mu^2>0 \text{ is nontrivial: } X=A\cos\mu x+B\sin\mu x,\ X(0)=A=0,\ X(L)=B\sin\mu L=0\Rightarrow \mu L=n\pi.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Eigenpairs: } \lambda_n=\left(\frac{n\pi}{L}\right)^2,\qquad X_n(x)=\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L},\qquad n=1,2,3,\dots} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Step 3 --- Coefficient formula.}\quad \text{Take } \langle\,\cdot\,,\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\rangle \text{ of } f=\sum_m c_m\sin\frac{m\pi x}{L};\ \text{orthogonality kills } m\neq n:\quad c_n=\frac{\displaystyle\int_0^L f(x)\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx}{\displaystyle\int_0^L \sin^2\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx}.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Denominator (normalization).}\quad \int_0^L \sin^2\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx=\int_0^L \frac{1-\cos\frac{2n\pi x}{L}}{2}\,dx=\frac12\left[x-\frac{L}{2n\pi}\sin\frac{2n\pi x}{L}\right]_0^L=\frac12\big(L-0\big)=\frac{L}{2}.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Numerator --- set up by parts.}\quad I_n=\int_0^L x\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx;\quad u=x,\ dv=\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx \ \Rightarrow\ du=dx,\ v=-\frac{L}{n\pi}\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{I_n=\Big[-\frac{Lx}{n\pi}\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}\Big]_0^L+\frac{L}{n\pi}\int_0^L \cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx \quad(\text{integration by parts}).} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Boundary term: } \Big[-\frac{Lx}{n\pi}\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}\Big]_0^L=-\frac{L^2}{n\pi}\cos n\pi-0=-\frac{L^2}{n\pi}(-1)^n=(-1)^{n+1}\frac{L^2}{n\pi}.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Remaining integral: } \frac{L}{n\pi}\int_0^L\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx=\frac{L}{n\pi}\Big[\frac{L}{n\pi}\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\Big]_0^L=\frac{L^2}{n^2\pi^2}\big(\sin n\pi-0\big)=0.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Combine: } I_n=(-1)^{n+1}\frac{L^2}{n\pi}+0=(-1)^{n+1}\frac{L^2}{n\pi}.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Coefficient: } c_n=\frac{I_n}{L/2}=(-1)^{n+1}\frac{L^2}{n\pi}\cdot\frac{2}{L}=(-1)^{n+1}\frac{2L}{n\pi}.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Step 4 --- Expansion.}\quad x=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}(-1)^{n+1}\frac{2L}{n\pi}\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}=\frac{2L}{\pi}\left(\sin\frac{\pi x}{L}-\tfrac12\sin\frac{2\pi x}{L}+\tfrac13\sin\frac{3\pi x}{L}-\cdots\right).} \]
\end{examplebox}

\begin{conceptbox}[Key Takeaway]
Orthogonality is the engine of every eigenfunction expansion. Because $\langle y_n,y_m\rangle=0$ for $n\neq m$, taking the inner product of $f=\sum c_m y_m$ with a single $y_n$ \textcolor{accentorange}{annihilates the entire sum but one term}, so each coefficient $c_n=\langle f,y_n\rangle/\langle y_n,y_n\rangle$ is computed \emph{independently} by one integral --- never by solving an infinite coupled system.
\end{conceptbox}

\begin{warningbox}[Common Pitfall]
Do not forget to \textcolor{warnred}{divide by the normalization} $\langle y_n,y_n\rangle$ --- here the $L/2$, not $1$. Writing $c_n=\int_0^L f\sin(n\pi x/L)\,dx$ alone is off by the factor $2/L$. And when a \textcolor{warnred}{weight $w(x)\neq 1$} is present, both integrals must carry it: $c_n=\int f y_n w\,dx \big/ \int y_n^2 w\,dx$.
\end{warningbox}

\vspace{0.8em}

% ---- Module 17.6 --------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Sturm--Liouville Theory}
\emph{The Sturm--Liouville form $(p\,y')'+(q+\lambda w)y=0$; regular S--L problems and their guarantees (real eigenvalues, $\lambda_n\to\infty$, $w$-orthogonal eigenfunctions, completeness); the Lagrange-identity proof of orthogonality; converting an equation to S--L form.}

\begin{formulabox}[The Sturm--Liouville Form]
\[ \big(p(x)\,y'\big)' + \big(q(x) + \lambda\,w(x)\big)y = 0, \qquad p,w>0 \ \text{on } [a,b], \quad \text{with homogeneous (separated) BCs.} \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Guarantees of a Regular S--L Problem]
\[ \text{(i) eigenvalues } \lambda_n \text{ real},\ \lambda_1<\lambda_2<\cdots\to\infty; \quad \text{(ii) } \langle y_n,y_m\rangle_w=0\ (n\neq m); \quad \text{(iii) } \{y_n\}\ \text{complete.} \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Putting an Equation in S--L Form]
\[ a_2 y''+a_1 y'+a_0 y+\lambda y=0 \ \xrightarrow{\ \mu=\frac1{a_2}e^{\int a_1/a_2\,dx}\ } \ (p y')'+(q+\lambda w)y=0,\ \ p=\mu a_2,\ w=\mu. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{methodbox}[Method --- Prove Eigenfunction Orthogonality]
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.6em]
    \item Take two eigenpairs $(\lambda_n,y_n)$ and $(\lambda_m,y_m)$, $\lambda_n\neq\lambda_m$: $(p y_n')'+(q+\lambda_n w)y_n=0$ and $(p y_m')'+(q+\lambda_m w)y_m=0$.
    \item Multiply the first by $y_m$ and the second by $y_n$, then subtract; the $q$-terms cancel, leaving $(\lambda_n-\lambda_m)w\,y_n y_m = y_n(p y_m')'-y_m(p y_n')'$.
    \item Recognize the right side as an exact derivative (Lagrange's identity): $y_n(p y_m')'-y_m(p y_n')' = \dfrac{d}{dx}\!\big[p\,(y_n y_m'-y_m y_n')\big]$.
    \item Integrate over $[a,b]$: $(\lambda_n-\lambda_m)\displaystyle\int_a^b w\,y_n y_m\,dx = \big[p(y_n y_m'-y_m y_n')\big]_a^b$.
    \item The separated homogeneous BCs make the bracket vanish at both ends; since $\lambda_n\neq\lambda_m$, conclude $\displaystyle\int_a^b y_n(x)y_m(x)\,w(x)\,dx=0$.
\end{enumerate}
\end{methodbox}

\begin{examplebox}[Worked Example --- The String Problem as a Sturm--Liouville Problem]
Cast $X''+\lambda X=0$, $X(0)=X(L)=0$ in Sturm--Liouville form, name $p,q,w$, and confirm the eigenfunctions $\sin(n\pi x/L)$ are $w$-orthogonal.
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Match the form: } (p X')'+(q+\lambda w)X=0 \ \text{with } X''+\lambda X=0 \Rightarrow p(x)=1,\ q(x)=0,\ w(x)=1.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Regularity: } p=1>0,\ w=1>0 \text{ on } [0,L] \text{ with separated BCs } X(0)=X(L)=0 \Rightarrow \text{a regular S--L problem.}} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Boundary term vanishes: } \big[p(y_n y_m'-y_m y_n')\big]_0^L=0 \ \text{since } \sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}=0 \text{ at } x=0,L.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Orthogonality confirmed: } \int_0^L \sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\sin\frac{m\pi x}{L}\,(1)\,dx=0 \ (n\neq m),\ =\frac{L}{2}\ (n=m).} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Conclusion: } \{\sin(n\pi x/L)\} \text{ is the complete, } w\text{-orthogonal eigenbasis predicted by S--L theory.}} \]
\end{examplebox}

\begin{conceptbox}[Key Takeaway]
Sturm--Liouville theory is the structural payoff of the unit: \emph{any} second-order linear eigenvalue problem can be written as $(p\,y')'+(q+\lambda w)y=0$, and once it is \textcolor{accentorange}{regular}, three gifts follow automatically --- the eigenvalues are \textcolor{accentorange}{real and unbounded}, the eigenfunctions are \textcolor{accentorange}{orthogonal with respect to the weight $w$}, and they form a \textcolor{accentorange}{complete basis}. That completeness is exactly what licenses the generalized Fourier expansion $f=\sum c_n y_n$.
\end{conceptbox}

\begin{warningbox}[Common Pitfall]
Eigenfunctions are orthogonal with respect to the \textcolor{warnred}{weight $w(x)$, not plain $dx$} --- whenever $w\neq 1$, the inner product and every coefficient integral must carry it. Also, the orthogonality proof leans on the boundary bracket $[p(y_n y_m'-y_m y_n')]_a^b$ vanishing; this requires \textcolor{warnred}{genuinely separated homogeneous BCs}, so the guarantees can fail for a problem that is not a regular S--L problem.
\end{warningbox}

\vspace{0.8em}

\clearpage
\phantomsection
\addcontentsline{toc}{section}{Practice Problems}
\section*{Practice Problems}

Work the following ten problems in order. They climb from telling an IVP from a BVP and solving simple two-point problems, through the full three-case eigenvalue analysis and the shooting method, up to the orthogonality integral, a complete eigenfunction expansion with every coefficient integral expanded, and the Sturm--Liouville orthogonality proof. Complete solutions for all problems follow.

\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=2em]
    \item \textbf{(IVP vs.\ BVP)} State what distinguishes a boundary value problem from an initial value problem, and identify which of $y(0)=1,\,y'(0)=2$ versus $y(0)=1,\,y(2)=5$ is which.

    \item \textbf{(Simple Dirichlet BVP)} Solve $y''=0$ on $[0,2]$ with $y(0)=1$, $y(2)=5$.

    \item \textbf{(Nonhomogeneous BVP)} Solve $y''=2$ on $[0,1]$ with $y(0)=0$, $y(1)=0$.

    \item \textbf{(Infinitely many solutions)} Show that $y''+y=0$ with $y(0)=0$, $y(\pi)=0$ has infinitely many solutions, and give their form.

    \item \textbf{(Eigenvalues, three cases)} For $y''+\lambda y=0$, $y(0)=y(L)=0$, carry out the $\lambda<0,\ \lambda=0,\ \lambda>0$ analysis and state $\lambda_n$ and $y_n$.

    \item \textbf{(Neumann spectrum)} Find the eigenvalues and eigenfunctions of $y''+\lambda y=0$ with $y'(0)=y'(L)=0$.

    \item \textbf{(Shooting method)} Solve $y''=y$ on $[0,1]$, $y(0)=0$, $y(1)=1$ by two shots and linear interpolation.

    \item \textbf{(Orthogonality integral)} Evaluate $\int_0^L \sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\sin\frac{m\pi x}{L}\,dx$ for $m\neq n$ and for $m=n$.

    \item \textbf{(Eigenfunction expansion)} Expand $f(x)=x$ on $[0,L]$ in the sine series $\sum_{n\ge 1}c_n\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}$, computing $c_n$ in full.

    \item \textbf{(Sturm--Liouville capstone)} Write $X''+\lambda X=0$, $X(0)=X(L)=0$ in S--L form (name $p,q,w$), then prove $\int_a^b y_n y_m w\,dx=0$ for $\lambda_n\neq\lambda_m$.

\end{enumerate}

\clearpage
\phantomsection
\addcontentsline{toc}{section}{Complete Solutions}
\section*{Complete Solutions}

\noindent\textbf{Solution 1.}\quad A BVP imposes its side conditions at \emph{two different points}, whereas an IVP stacks all of them at a single point. Hence
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{y(0)=1,\ y'(0)=2 \ \text{(both at } x=0\text{)} \Rightarrow \textbf{IVP}; \qquad y(0)=1,\ y(2)=5 \ \text{(at } x=0,2\text{)} \Rightarrow \textbf{BVP}.} \]
Unlike the IVP, the BVP carries no automatic existence--uniqueness guarantee.

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 2.}\quad The general solution of $y''=0$ is $y=c_1+c_2 x$. Imposing the endpoints,
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{y(0)=c_1=1; \qquad y(2)=1+2c_2=5 \Rightarrow c_2=2.} \]
Unique solution: $y(x)=1+2x$.

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 3.}\quad Integrate twice: $y=x^2+c_2 x+c_1$. Then
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{y(0)=c_1=0; \qquad y(1)=1+c_2=0 \Rightarrow c_2=-1.} \]
Unique solution: $y(x)=x^2-x$.

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 4.}\quad The general solution is $y=A\cos x+B\sin x$. The conditions give
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{y(0)=A=0; \qquad y(\pi)=A\cos\pi+B\sin\pi=-A=0,} \]
which is satisfied for \emph{every} $B$. Thus $y=B\sin x$ is a solution for all $B$ --- infinitely many (here $\lambda=1$ is an eigenvalue).

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 5.}\quad For $\lambda<0$ and $\lambda=0$ the BCs force $y\equiv 0$. For $\lambda=\mu^2>0$, $y=A\cos\mu x+B\sin\mu x$ with $y(0)=A=0$, so $y=B\sin\mu x$ and $y(L)=B\sin\mu L=0$:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\sin\mu L=0 \Rightarrow \mu L=n\pi \Rightarrow \lambda_n=\left(\frac{n\pi}{L}\right)^2,\quad y_n=\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L},\quad n=1,2,3,\dots} \]

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 6.}\quad With $y=A\cos\mu x+B\sin\mu x$, $y'=\mu(-A\sin\mu x+B\cos\mu x)$. Then $y'(0)=\mu B=0\Rightarrow B=0$, and $y'(L)=-\mu A\sin\mu L=0$:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\sin\mu L=0 \Rightarrow \lambda_n=\left(\frac{n\pi}{L}\right)^2,\quad y_n=\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L},\quad n=0,1,2,\dots} \]
The Neumann case keeps $n=0$: $\lambda_0=0$ with constant eigenfunction $y_0=1$.

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 7.}\quad Recast as $y''=y$, $y(0)=0$, $y'(0)=s$, with exact IVP solution $y(x;s)=s\sinh x$. Two shots:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{F(0)=0-1=-1; \quad F(1)=\sinh 1-1\approx 0.1752; \quad s^\star=\frac{1}{\sinh 1}\approx 0.8509.} \]
Final solution: $y(x)=\dfrac{\sinh x}{\sinh 1}$, exact since the problem is linear.

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 8.}\quad Use $\sin\alpha\sin\beta=\tfrac12[\cos(\alpha-\beta)-\cos(\alpha+\beta)]$. For $m\neq n$ both cosine integrals vanish over $[0,L]$, giving $0$. For $m=n$,
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\int_0^L \sin^2\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx=\int_0^L\frac{1-\cos\frac{2n\pi x}{L}}{2}\,dx=\frac{L}{2}.} \]
So the integral is $0$ for $m\neq n$ and $L/2$ for $m=n$.

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 9.}\quad With weight $w=1$, $c_n=\big(\int_0^L x\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}dx\big)\big/\big(\int_0^L\sin^2\frac{n\pi x}{L}dx\big)$. The denominator is $L/2$; integrating the numerator by parts,
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\int_0^L x\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx=\Big[-\frac{Lx}{n\pi}\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}\Big]_0^L+\frac{L}{n\pi}\int_0^L\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx=(-1)^{n+1}\frac{L^2}{n\pi}+0.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{c_n=\frac{(-1)^{n+1}L^2/(n\pi)}{L/2}=(-1)^{n+1}\frac{2L}{n\pi} \ \Rightarrow\ x=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}(-1)^{n+1}\frac{2L}{n\pi}\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}.} \]

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 10.}\quad Matching $(pX')'+(q+\lambda w)X=0$ to $X''+\lambda X=0$ gives $p=1,\ q=0,\ w=1$ (regular, since $p,w>0$ with separated BCs). For the proof, multiply $(py_n')'+\lambda_n w y_n=0$ by $y_m$, the $m$-equation by $y_n$, subtract, and integrate:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{(\lambda_n-\lambda_m)\int_a^b w\,y_n y_m\,dx=\big[p\,(y_n y_m'-y_m y_n')\big]_a^b=0,} \]
where the bracket vanishes by the homogeneous BCs. Since $\lambda_n\neq\lambda_m$, $\int_a^b y_n y_m w\,dx=0$.

\medskip

\begin{conceptbox}[Unit Key Takeaway]
One thread ties the unit together: \textcolor{accentorange}{boundary conditions create a spectrum, and orthogonality turns that spectrum into a basis}. Splitting the side data across two endpoints can leave a linear problem with no solution, one, or infinitely many --- the last case is where the \textcolor{accentorange}{eigenvalues $\lambda_n=(n\pi/L)^2$} and \textcolor{accentorange}{eigenfunctions $\sin(n\pi x/L)$} live. Those eigenfunctions are \textcolor{accentorange}{orthogonal with respect to a weight $w$}, so any reasonable $f$ expands as $f=\sum c_n y_n$ with each $c_n=\langle f,y_n\rangle/\langle y_n,y_n\rangle$ a single integral. The \textcolor{accentorange}{Sturm--Liouville form $(p\,y')'+(q+\lambda w)y=0$} is the master frame that guarantees real eigenvalues, weighted orthogonality, and a complete functional basis.
\end{conceptbox}

\end{document}
